title: Fermion sign problem and the structure of Lee-Yang zeros. II. Finite temperature results for a model system without interactions
authors: Ran-Chen He, Jia-Xi Zeng, Shu Yang, Cong Wang, Qi-Jun Ye, Xin-Zheng Li
abstract: Beyond the analysis of the Lee-Yang (LY) zero of $ξ$ at $0$ K presented by our previous work [He et. al. Phys. Rev. E 113, 24115 (2026)], it is important but intricate to understand how these zeros evolve with temperature ($T$). Here, we use an analytically solvable noninteracting one-dimensional particle-on-a-ring model to address this. We determine the trajectories of these zeros and analyze how their evolution with $T$ reshapes the analytic structure of the partition function. In particular, the zero originating from $ξ=-1$ at $T=0$ remains close to $-1$ at low $T$, where it governs the sign factor and strongly constrains continuation along the real $ξ$ axis. This explains why both direct extrapolation and implicit schemes such as contour-based fitting can fail in the low-$T$ regime, even at high fitting order, while becoming reasonable again once the relevant zeros move away at higher $T$s. Furthermore, based on the polynomial structure of the partition function, we propose a new fitting strategy for low-$T$ fermionic properties. The key is to first obtain reliable high-$T$ fermionic properties by continuing sign-problem-free data in $ξ\in[0,1]$ to $ξ=-1$, and then extend this information toward lower $T$ through $T$-fitting of the $ξ$-independent remainder $φ(β)=Z_{\text{F}}$. These results provide a solvable benchmark for diagnosing the validity of analytic continuation and suggest a possible route toward treating more realistic interacting fermionic systems.
summary: The paper tackles the challenging issue of calculating fermionic properties at low temperatures where standard analytic continuation techniques fail due to Lee-Yang zeros. Using a solvable 1D model, the authors map out how these zeros move with temperature and propose a reliable two-step fitting strategy that bypasses the singularities, offering a benchmark for diagnosing computational methods in quantum statistical mechanics.
main_problem: The primary goal is understanding how Lee-Yang (LY) zeros of the exchange parity $\xi$ evolve with temperature ($T$) to diagnose the limitations of analytic continuation methods for calculating fermionic properties.
main_result: The authors propose a two-step strategy: first, extrapolating reliable high-$T$ data from sign-problem-free regions ($\xi \in [0, 1]$) to $\xi = -1$, and second, performing a temperature fit on the resulting fermionic partition function remainder $\phi(\beta)$.
method: The analysis uses an analytically solvable noninteracting 1D particle-on-a-ring model to derive explicit polynomial forms for the partition function $Z(\beta, \xi)$ and track the trajectories of its zeros in the complex $\xi$-plane.
model_or_system: The system is a noninteracting one-dimensional particle-on-a-ring model with $N$ particles. The physics is governed by the partition function $Z(\beta, \xi)$, which depends on temperature ($\beta$) and the exchange parity parameter $\xi$.
key_observables: Lee-Yang (LY) zeros of $\xi$, the free energy $F(T, \xi)$, and the sign factor $\langle s \rangle_B$ that governs fermionic contributions.
important_parameters: Temperature ($T$) or inverse temperature ($\beta$), the exchange parity parameter ($\xi$), and the number of particles ($N$).
main_assumptions: The initial analysis is restricted to noninteracting systems, serving as a solvable benchmark for diagnosing continuation methods.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the trajectories of LY zeros in the complex $\xi$-plane as $T$ changes (showing movement off the real axis at high $T$), and demonstrate the failure of direct extrapolation versus the success of the proposed two-step temperature fitting strategy.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the model using an analytically solvable noninteracting system. It then analyzes how the trajectories of LY zeros dictate the analyticity of thermodynamic quantities. Finally, it proposes and validates a robust two-step procedure for calculating low-$T$ fermionic properties by combining high-$T$ extrapolation with temperature fitting.
why_interesting: This work provides crucial theoretical diagnostics for computational methods tackling the fermion sign problem in many-body physics, suggesting a generalizable protocol applicable to more complex interacting systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The LY zeros of ξ at different Ts for the nonin- teracting N = 4 system on a 1D ring. For N = 4, there are three LY zeros of ξ and thus three trajectories (dotted lines connecting the same zero at different Ts), shown in the background. We identify the zeros by continuously tracking their T-dependent evolution, and label them in red, orange, and blue colors. The temperature step is ∆T = 0.01 from T = 0.25 to T = 1, in units of h2/(2mkBL2). The zeros cor- responding to (a) T = 0.25, (b) T = 0.278, and (c) T = 0.714 are highlighted as larger crosses, with black arrows indicating the directions of motion as T increases. For low-T cases, the zero originating from −1 remains very close...

FIG. 2. The LY zeros of ξ at different Ts for the noninter- acting N = 5 system on a 1D ring. The plotting conventions are the same as in Fig. 1. Here, four zeros are shown (red, orange, blue, and purple), with ∆T = 0.01 from T = 0.2 to T = 3. The zeros at (a) T = 0.2, (b) T = 0.252, (c) T = 1, (d) T = 1.65, and (e) T = 1.8 are highlighted as larger crosses. Similar to Fig. 1, arrows indicate motion with increasing T, except for the low-T zero originating from −1, whose arrow is omitted due to Eq. (3).

FIG. 3. (a) Curves of Z(ξ) on the real ξ axis for N = 4. LY zeros of ξ are exactly the intersections with Z = 0. At T = 0, there are three roots, as ξ = −1, −1/2, −1/3. As T increases, two neighboring real roots merge into a double root at ξ ≈−0.45 (around T = 0.278), and then leave the real axis as a complex-conjugate pair, so that only one real zero remains. (b) Curves of Z(ξ) for N = 5. Consistent with the parity constraint discussed in the text, the four roots initialized at ξ = −1, −1/2, −1/3, −1/4 leave the real axis in two pairs; consequently, no real zero remains at sufficiently high T (here T ≳1.2).

FIG. 4. Free energy F(T, ξ) versus ξ for (a) N = 4 and (b) N = 5, with LY zeros depicted at the corresponding Ts (dif- ferent colors). In accordance with the main text, zeros on the real axis correspond to nonanalytic points (sharp diver- gences), while zeros close to the real axis still distort the free energy profile. The unit of free energy is h2/(2mL2).

FIG. 5. Sign factor ⟨s⟩B and LY zero z1 for N = 4 and 5 on a logarithmic scale. The blue curves show the contribution from z1 as temperature increases, and they exhibit behavior very similar to the red curves, which represent the sign factor ⟨s⟩B on the same logarithmic scale. The temperature unit is h2/(2mkBL2).
